302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate of 41.3% in elderly dengue patients justifies the use of any available laboratory test for early risk stratification
Evidence: The in-hospital CFR was 41.3% (31 patients), and these patients had a median ICU stay of 4 (IQR: 1–8) days.
The leading cause of fatality was multiple organ failure (14/31, 45.2%), and nine of these 14 patients had overt bleeding.
302.Rationale: While the high case fatality rate underscores the severity of the condition, it does not directly support the use of a single laboratory test over multi-parameter scoring systems. The justification for using a specific test must be based on its independent predictive value, not just the overall severity of the disease.

302.Option: Prolonged APTT at ICU admission remains an independent predictor of in-hospital fatality even after accounting for multi-parameter severity scores
Evidence: In the multivariate analysis, ... and initial APTT longer than 48 s (HR 3.91 [1.69–9.07]) were independent predictive factors for in-hospital fatality after adjusting for gender, cardiac arrest before ICU admission, respiratory failure at ICU admission, presence of AKI at ICU admission and initial APTT longer than 48 s.

Moreover, APTT prolongation > 48 s remained to be an independent predictive factor for in-hospital fatality after adjusting for APACHE II score (HR: 3.34, P = 0.006), and was marginally significant after adjusting for SOFA score (HR: 2.43, P = 0.053).
302.Rationale: The evidence directly supports that APTT prolongation is an independent predictor of in-hospital fatality even when accounting for multi-parameter severity scores like APACHE II and SOFA. This suggests that they are not independent predictors and may not add unique value beyond simpler tests like APTT.

302.Option: APTT prolongation is more sensitive than APACHE II and SOFA scores for detecting early signs of organ failure in elderly dengue patients
Evidence: The area under the ROC curve (AUC) of APTT was 0.76 (95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.64–0.88), APACHE II score 0.84 (0.75–0.93), and SOFA score 0.85 (0.77–0.94). Though there was no significant difference between APTT and two scores (APTT vs. APACHE II, P = 0.27; APTT vs. SOFA, P = 0.16), AUCs of APACHE II and SOFA scores, which both were a constellation of multiple clinical and laboratory variables, were higher than that of APTT.
302.Rationale: The AUCs for APACHE II and SOFA scores were higher than that of APTT, indicating that they are more accurate overall. Sensitivity alone is not sufficient to justify the use of a single test over multi-parameter systems, especially when the latter have higher overall accuracy.

This suggests that it does not add independent predictive value beyond other factors like APTT prolongation.

306.Option: The use of a quantitative ELISA assay to measure circulating filarial antigen levels, enabling detection of changes in adult worm burden over time
Evidence: The TropBio ELISA is measuring the circulating filarial antigen of the adult filarial worm. Values above OD 0.35 are considered as positive, with low positive results between OD 0.35 and 0.8, intermediate positive results between OD 0.8 and 1.0, and high positive results above the OD of 1.0. Most filarial infected individuals in 2009 showed high positive results, reflecting a high adult worm burden. In 2019 infected individuals had low positive ODs indicating a low number of adult worms.

The mean OD values by age group declined from 0.55, 0.63, 0.74 to 0.34, 0.44, 0.44 (38%, 30%, 40.5%) between 2009 and 2010/11, then to 0.08, 0.10, 0.11 (85.4%, 84.1%, 85.1%) in 2019 in participants within the age group range of 14 to <25, 25 to <45 and 45 to < = 66 years respectively (Fig 4A). The mean OD of CFA positive individuals was 1.55 (SD 0.70) in 2009, 1.11 (SD 0.56), in 2010/11 and 0.49 (SD 0.10) in 2019.

Matching the OD values of the TropBio ELISA from the previous and the current study we found that 18 of the 22 remaining infected individuals (81.8%) were still CFA seropositive, but at the same time showed a significantly reduced antigen load. This shows that the treatment has an effect even among the infected individuals (Figs 3 and 4). It can be concluded that these individuals were reached by the MDA programme but that the treatment efficacy was not optimal or treatment incomplete. This is a benefit of using a quantitative ELISA instead of a qualitative measurement.
306.Rationale: While the quantitative ELISA provides more detailed information about the magnitude of infection and adult worm burden, it does not directly address the causal relationship between MDA and the observed reduction. The ELISA is a tool for measurement, not a methodological feature that strengthens causal inference. The study already uses this method, but it is not the feature that most strengthens the confidence in attributing the reduction to MDA.

306.Option: The inclusion of a control group from a non-treatment region to compare changes in filarial prevalence over time
Evidence: The study participants were again visited in 2019, after seven years of annual treatment and additional three years of surveillance. Our results show a dramatic reduction in LF prevalence from 35.1% in 2009, to 1.7% in 2019 among the individuals aged 14 to 65 years. The intriguing feature in this cohort is that the very same people have been visited in 2009, 2010/11 and 2019, thus excluding a sampling bias of this study. Admittedly this approach adds an age bias instead, but since older age is associated with higher LF-prevalence, this age bias should lead to an increased prevalence, while our study shows a greatly reduced prevalence.
306.Rationale: The study does not mention the inclusion of a control group from a non-treatment region. The absence of a control group is a limitation, but the question asks which feature most strengthens the confidence in attributing the reduction to MDA. The inclusion of a control group would help, but since it is not part of the study, this option is incorrect.

306.Option: The use of a fixed cohort with repeated measurements over time, which controls for both sampling bias and age-related confounding
Evidence: The study participants were again visited in 2019, after seven years of annual treatment and additional three years of surveillance. Our results show a dramatic reduction in LF prevalence from 35.1% in 2009, to 1.7% in 2019 among the individuals aged 14 to 65 years. The intriguing feature in this cohort is that the very same people have been visited in 2009, 2010/11 and 2019, thus excluding a sampling bias of this study. Admittedly this approach adds an age bias instead, but since older age is associated with higher LF-prevalence, this age bias should lead to an increased prevalence, while our study shows a greatly reduced prevalence.
306.Rationale: The use of a fixed cohort with repeated measurements over time is a key methodological feature that strengthens the confidence in attributing the observed reduction to MDA. By following the same individuals over time, the study controls for sampling bias and allows for the observation of changes within the same individuals, which helps to isolate the effect of MDA from other factors. The fact that the same people were visited in 2009, 2010/11, and 2019, and that the reduction in prevalence was observed despite an age bias that would have increased prevalence, supports the conclusion that the MDA program is the most likely cause of the reduction.

306.Option: The statistical adjustment for age, sex, and occupation in multivariable regression models to control for potential confounders
Evidence: Multi-variable analysis confirmed that W. bancrofti infection was associated with increasing age, male sex and filarial infection during the previous study (Table 3).

By controlling for these potential confounders, the study can more accurately estimate the effect of MDA on filarial infection. The multivariable analysis confirmed that the observed associations were independent of these factors, which supports the conclusion that the reduction in prevalence is due to MDA rather than other confounding variables.

308.Option: The combination of waning insecticidal efficacy and reduced use of complementary protective behaviors likely led to increased dengue exposure despite high curtain usage
Evidence: The results indicate that participants living in the intervention arm were not better protected from DENV exposure than those in the control arm, despite the widespread use of ITCs.

Three factors, however, could have contributed to this unexpected result. First, despite being fabricated from a material that was expected to retain high insecticide levels over the course of several years, the ITCs quickly lost insecticidal efficacy, leading to the need for mass re-treatment only 1-year after they had been originally deployed.

Second, a contributing factor may have been a false sense of security amongst the participants in the intervention arm; i.e., perhaps the presence of the highly visible ITCs created a belief in their protective power among the treated households, who subsequently did not employ any additional measures typically used to avoid exposure to mosquito bites.

Participants that had received ITCs commented that when the curtains were first hung, the household reduced their use of mosquito repellents and stopped fumigating because they felt that it was no longer necessary due to the presence of the ITCs.
308.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies two key factors: 1) the ITCs lost insecticidal efficacy quickly, requiring re-treatment after only 1 year, and 2) participants in the intervention arm reduced their use of other protective measures (repellents, fumigation) due to a false sense of security from the ITCs. These two factors together explain the increased dengue seroconversion despite high curtain usage and initial entomological improvements.

308.Option: The initial entomological improvements observed in the intervention arm were not sustained due to the failure of insecticide-treated curtains to maintain efficacy over time, leading to increased dengue transmission
Evidence: The results indicate that participants living in the intervention arm were not better protected from DENV exposure than those in the control arm, despite the widespread use of ITCs.

Three factors, however, could have contributed to this unexpected result. First, despite being fabricated from a material that was expected to retain high insecticide levels over the course of several years, the ITCs quickly lost insecticidal efficacy, leading to the need for mass re-treatment only 1-year after they had been originally deployed.
The 9-month serosurvey occurred before the ITCs were re-treated, which means that many were operating sub-optimally and could have potentially had reduced protective effectiveness.
308.Rationale: While the passage does mention that the ITCs lost insecticidal efficacy over time, it also explicitly states that the increased seroconversion in the intervention arm was due to a combination of factors, including the waning efficacy and the reduced use of complementary protective behaviors. This option only addresses one of the two key factors and therefore is not the most complete explanation.

308.Option: The re-treatment of insecticide-treated curtains with deltamethrin likely disrupted the protective effect by causing a temporary reduction in insecticidal activity, increasing dengue exposure during the follow-up period
Evidence: Cone bioassays after re-treatment with KO-Tab 123 showed that the curtains did not immediately recover to the 100% bioefficacy observed at baseline. At 1 month following re-treatment (January 2011), average mortality for 36 ITCs was 74.5% (range: 48%-94%). By 9-months following re-treatment (August 2011) average mortality from 9 tested ITCs was 97.2% (range: 87.5%-100%).
The 9-month serosurvey occurred before the ITCs were re-treated, which means that many were operating sub-optimally and could have potentially had reduced protective effectiveness.
308.Rationale: The passage indicates that the re-treatment did not immediately restore full insecticidal efficacy, but the 9-month serosurvey occurred before the ITCs were re-treated. Therefore, the re-treatment itself was not the cause of increased dengue exposure during the follow-up period. The increased exposure occurred before the re-treatment took place.

308.Option: The emergence of deltamethrin resistance in the local mosquito population likely reduced the effectiveness of insecticide-treated curtains, leading to increased dengue transmission despite high usage
Evidence: Bioassays detected an increase in resistance to deltamethrin in the local Ae. aegypti population over the course of the study. Resistance was first detected in early 2011, after ITCs had been deployed for over a year, soon after they were re-treated with deltamethrin.

This initial detection of resistance was worrying, because it could indicate that low concentrations of insecticide on ITCs that were not adequately loaded with deltamethrin (the reason why the re-treatment was carried out) had already begun to select for deltamethrin resistance in the local mosquito population.
308.Rationale: While the passage does mention the emergence of deltamethrin resistance, it also indicates that this resistance was detected after the ITCs had been deployed for over a year and after they were re-treated. The increased dengue seroconversion was observed before the detection of resistance, suggesting that resistance was not the primary factor contributing to the increased transmission.

Evidence: The final clinical response rate (94.2%) is similar to the final clinical response rate observed in the Phase III (94.2% final response) and Phase IV (94.6% final response) studies of PMIM in India [4,5].

The 6-month observation after treatment was the only practically feasible way to assess a cure in this study because splenic aspiration or other invasive procedures were not appropriate to conduct at the clinical study centers.

It also does not adjust for potential confounders or provide a statistical test for trends over time. 317.Rationale: The passage discusses how T. cruzi delays the moulting of triatomines, which in turn delays their next bloodmeal. This delay benefits the parasite by allowing it to reach the infective stage before the vector feeds again. However, the option suggests that the duration of the feeding cycle determines the balance, whereas the passage emphasizes the delay in moulting (and thus feeding) as a strategy for the parasite, not the duration of the feeding cycle itself. The study also shows that temperature affects moulting time, which is a more critical factor in the balance between parasite fitness and vector survival.

Infection with T. cruzi was found to accelerate mortality in the two intermediate temperatures, 24 (P = 0.02) and 27°C (P = 0.0001) (Fig. 1F & G), but not at 21 or 30°C (Fig. 1E & H).

317.Option: Temperature-dependent parasite growth rate relative to vector thermal preference determines the trade-off between transmission success and vector survival
Evidence: R. prolixus prefers temperatures of 25.0–25.4°C when offered a choice and performs best around these temperatures [40]. Furthermore, temperatures in the sylvatic ecotopes in which this insect is to be found oscillate closely around 25°C [41].

The population growth of T. cruzi in vitro increased consistently with increasing temperature (Fig. 2; p<0.0001 for temperature effect). The best-fit regression of growth rates against temperature (Fig. 2B) was not curved, so peak growth would have occurred at or above 30°C.
Infection with T. cruzi was found to accelerate mortality in the two intermediate temperatures, 24 (P = 0.02) and 27°C (P = 0.0001) (Fig. 1F & G), but not at 21 or 30°C (Fig. 1E & H).

It is well known that high temperatures promote an increase in the metabolism of insects (reviewed by [37]). Therefore, an increased mortality would be expected in starved insects submitted to higher temperatures, as already seen in previous studies [38]. According to the effect of temperature on T. cruzi growth in culture media, the mortality of infected insects would be expected to occur as a consequence of large parasite populations developed at higher temperatures. Nevertheless, there were no differences in mortality rates between infected and healthy insects kept at 30°C. This was probably a result of a lack of nutritional resources for parasite development in starved insects.

Given these insects are able to display temperature preferences [40, 42–45], we might expect them, when infected with T. cruzi, to alter their thermal preferences. All of these factors are liable to affect T. cruzi transmission dynamics and ultimately, epidemiology.
317.Rationale: The passage provides strong evidence that temperature plays a critical role in the balance between T. cruzi growth and R. prolixus survival. The vector prefers temperatures around 25°C, which is close to the temperatures (24-27°C) where T. cruzi growth leads to increased vector mortality. At 30°C, while parasite growth is highest, the lack of nutritional resources in starved insects prevents the same level of mortality. This suggests that the temperature-dependent growth rate of the parasite, relative to the vector's thermal preference, is a key factor in determining the trade-off between transmission success and vector survival. The study's findings directly support this option as the most critical factor in the natural setting.

331.Option: Using a fluorescence-based detection system with a 450 nm emission filter allows for real-time monitoring of NADPH consumption and reduces interference from non-fluorescent compounds
Evidence: Because NADPH is naturally fluorescent, emitting at ∼450 nm, while its oxidized counterpart NADP is not, we switched the detection platform for the coupled reaction from absorbance to fluorescence

The 450 nm emission filter is used to detect NADPH fluorescence, but does not inherently distinguish between true biological activity and artifacts from other fluorescent compounds.

333.Option: Use a combination of multiple parasitological methods and antigen detection to establish a composite reference standard
Evidence: The inclusion of additional parasitological methods for schistosome diagnosis resulted in the detection of a much higher number of infected individuals within the study population (Table 3). The apparent prevalence rose from 20.4 to 29.9%, when the number of KK slides was increased from two to 14 slides, or to 38.3%, when we used two slides prepared from each of the three fecal samples. Other parasitological methods that used a higher amount of fecal matter, such as the saline gradient and Helmintex, also detected a higher number of S. mansoni-infected individuals (Table 3). Overall, and taking into consideration the results of all the parasitological methods (reference standard), the prevalence of intestinal schistosomiasis reached 45.9%, which represents a 2.3 times increase in relation to the WHO’s recommended standard KK procedure.
The best performance of the KK method due to an increased number of examined slides (14 slides) or increased sampling effort (three fecal samples) is shown in Fig 3.

The combination of two KK slides from one fecal sample (1SPL K1-K2) with Helmintex or of six slides from three fecal samples (SPL1-3 K1-K2) with POC-CCA resulted in the highest prevalence rates (45.4% and 60.3%) and highest sensitivity rates (90.0% and 88.3%) (Table 6).
333.Rationale: The study demonstrates that using a composite reference standard combining multiple parasitological methods (e.g., multiple Kato-Katz slides, saline gradient, Helmintex) and antigen detection (POC-CCA) significantly increases the sensitivity of detecting S. mansoni infections, especially in low-endemicity areas where parasite loads are typically low. This approach minimizes misclassification bias by leveraging the strengths of each method to capture a more accurate estimate of true prevalence.

Evidence: The POC-CCA identified 108 out of a total of 228 individuals as infected, which resulted in a prevalence of 47.4% and a sensitivity of 64.9%, when compared with the reference standard (18 Kato-Katz slides, saline gradient, and Helmintex). The sensitivity of the POC-CCA was superior to the saline gradient and comparable to the results obtained with six KK slides from three fecal samples. However, the kappa index of the urine test was considerably lower than that obtained with the other parasitological tests (Table 5).
The main shortcoming of the rapid urine test (POC-CCA) was a low concordance with the reference standard, since we found 30 and 35% of false positive and false negative results, respectively.
333.Rationale: While the POC-CCA has higher sensitivity than the standard KK method, it still has a significant rate of false positives and false negatives (30% and 35%, respectively) when compared to a comprehensive reference standard. Relying solely on POC-CCA as the primary diagnostic tool would introduce misclassification bias due to its lower concordance with the true infection status.

333.Option: Increase the number of Kato-Katz slides examined from one fecal sample to 14 to improve sensitivity and reduce false negatives
Evidence: The sensitivity of the KK method increased from 41.4% with two slides from one fecal sample to up to 66.7% with six slides from three fecal samples. Even using the superior version of the KK technique, which involves analyzing two slides from three different fecal samples, we still missed more than one third of the infected population.
333.Rationale: While increasing the number of Kato-Katz slides from one fecal sample to 14 improves sensitivity compared to the standard two-slide method, it is still insufficient to detect all infected individuals, especially those with low parasite loads. The study shows that even with 14 slides from one sample, the sensitivity remains around 60%, and more than one-third of infected individuals are still missed.

333.Option: Use a single fecal sample with six Kato-Katz slides and one POC-CCA test to balance sensitivity and operational feasibility

For H. contortus, although both hard and soft selective sweeps are present there is a predominance of hard sweeps.
On farms 95 (N. Yorkshire) and 110 (Kent) a single resistant haplotype was detected characteristic of a classical "hard" selective sweep.
On farms 37 (Gloucester), 86 (Middlesex) and 101 (E. Sussex), although multiple resistance haplotypes are present, one of these predominates at high frequency in each case.
However, in contrast, on farm 54 (Devon) there are two discrete resistance haplotypes at almost equal frequency characteristic of a "soft" selective sweep.
For T. circumcincta, the selective sweeps are generally much softer than for H. contortus.
The most extreme example is farm 37 (Gloucester) on which there are a remarkable ten different resistance haplotypes present at similar frequency.
This is consistent with the overall higher level of genetic diversity of T. circumcincta providing a greater supply of resistance mutations.
Even so, a predominantly hard selective sweep is present on farm 95 (N. Yorkshire) a single haplotype predominates at high frequency.
The striking differences we see in the selective sweeps between farms is consistent with the hypothesis that recurrent mutations occur.

This is most obvious for T. circumcincta; since there is little genetic sub-structuring of this parasite between farms (based on neutral microsatellite markers), the standing genetic variation should be similar on each farm.

Consequently, if the standing genetic variation were the only source of resistance mutations, one would predict that broadly similar selective sweeps and resistance haplotypes would be present on each farm.
However, this is clearly not the case.

For example, T. circumcincta populations on farms 37 and 95 have little genetic differentiation based on neutral markers (FST; -0.0023) but have dramatically different selective sweeps; ten different resistance haplotypes for farm 37 and a four different resistance haplotypes on farm 95 without a single haplotype being shared.
Likewise for H. contortus, for farms 37 and 95 there are completely different resistance haplotypes present in spite of very little genetic differentiation for neutral markers (FST 0.0087).
The phylogenetic relationship of the resistance haplotypes is also consistent with a model of multiple independent origins of benzimidazole resistance mutations across the UK.
In the case of H. contortus, there are at least 4 independent origins of the resistance haplotypes present on seven UK farms and an even larger number for T. circumcincta.
In both cases, with the exception of the P198L substitution, our analysis detects few recent recombination events consistent with mutations appearing on divergent susceptible haplotypes.
Given the large population sizes of trichostrongylid nematodes, we should not be surprised that independent resistance mutations can repeatedly arise and become selected in these organisms.
The population parameter = 2Ne describes the rate at which new mutations arise in a population where Ne = effective population size and = the per site mutations rate.
It has been suggested that if is greater than 0.1, then soft selective sweeps are likely to occur.

A population size of 107 would be predicted to be sufficient for recurrent mutations to repeatedly arise and produce soft selective sweeps if the mutation rate is similar to that of C. elegans (10–8 mutations per base per generation).
We, and others, have previously pointed out that a single flock of a few hundred sheep can potentially contaminate pastures with billions of eggs every week.

Although, the majority of these larvae on the pasture never make it into the host, it is very likely that Ne for these organisms is more than adequate for soft selective sweeps to occur even at the individual farm level.
350.Rationale: The passage provides strong evidence that resistance mutations have arisen independently multiple times across different farms, even when there is minimal genetic differentiation between the parasite populations. This is supported by the observation that farms with similar neutral genetic backgrounds have vastly different resistance haplotypes, indicating that the mutations are not derived from a common ancestor but rather from recurrent de novo mutations. The high haplotype diversity and the lack of shared haplotypes between farms further support this conclusion. The study's findings align with the hypothesis that large population sizes and high mutation rates lead to recurrent mutations, which are then selected for in response to drug pressure.

The key evidence is that farms with similar neutral genetic backgrounds have vastly different resistance haplotypes, which would not be expected if the mutations were pre-existing. 351.Option: High levels of systemic IL-10 are most strongly associated with severe urogenital pathology and may mask the true extent of Th1 immune suppression in individuals with high parasite burden
Evidence: In a study in Kenyan individuals with Sh infection and concomitant bladder pathology assessed by ultrasonography reported a 12-fold higher serum TNF-α production at baseline and 13-fold higher production in response to schistosome and purified protein derivative antigen stimulation than in persons with Sh infection without bladder pathology [74].

In a Gabonese cohort, Sh urinary egg burden correlated inversely with IFN-γ levels in PBMCs after AWA or SEA stimulation from individuals who developed Sh reinfection two years after PZQ treatment, suggesting a lower pro-inflammatory immune environment in those with the highest Sh burden [75].

In a Senegalese cohort, women with Sh infection produced lower levels of TNF-α and IFN-γ following SEA stimulation of PBMCs, while they produced more TGF-β and IL-10 and had higher expression of the apoptosis marker nuclear matrix protein, compared to men with Sh infection with comparable egg burdens and schistosome circulating anodic antigen (CAA) levels [83].
351.Rationale: The passage discusses the role of IL-10 in modulating immune responses during Sh infection. While IL-10 is an anti-inflammatory cytokine that can suppress Th1 responses, the evidence suggests that severe urogenital pathology is more strongly associated with pro-inflammatory cytokines like TNF-α rather than IL-10. The study in Kenyan individuals with bladder pathology showed higher TNF-α levels, and the Senegalese study found that women with Sh infection produced more IL-10 but lower Th1 cytokines. However, the inverse correlation between Sh egg burden and IFN-γ in the Gabonese cohort suggests that high parasite burden may suppress Th1 responses, but this is not directly linked to IL-10 levels as the primary factor for severe pathology.

351.Option: High parasite burden is most strongly associated with severe urogenital pathology and may confound systemic immune response measurements by introducing a strong pro-inflammatory signal independent of infection intensity
Evidence: In a study in Kenyan individuals with Sh infection and concomitant bladder pathology assessed by ultrasonography reported a 12-fold higher serum TNF-α production at baseline and 13-fold higher production in response to schistosome and purified protein derivative antigen stimulation than in persons with Sh infection without bladder pathology [74].

In a Gabonese cohort, Sh urinary egg burden correlated inversely with IFN-γ levels in PBMCs after AWA or SEA stimulation from individuals who developed Sh reinfection two years after PZQ treatment, suggesting a lower pro-inflammatory immune environment in those with the highest Sh burden [75].

In a study in Kenyan individuals with Sh infection and concomitant bladder pathology assessed by ultrasonography reported a 12-fold higher serum TNF-α production at baseline and 13-fold higher production in response to schistosome and purified protein derivative antigen stimulation than in persons with Sh infection without bladder pathology [74].
351.Rationale: The passage indicates that high parasite burden is associated with lower Th1 cytokine responses (e.g., IFN-γ) rather than a strong pro-inflammatory signal. The Kenyan study found higher TNF-α in those with bladder pathology, but this is a specific finding related to pathology rather than a general confounder due to infection intensity. The Gabonese study showed an inverse correlation between egg burden and IFN-γ, suggesting that high parasite burden may suppress Th1 responses, not introduce a pro-inflammatory signal independent of infection intensity.

351.Option: Female sex is most strongly associated with severe urogenital pathology and may lead to overestimation of systemic immune activation due to hormonal influences on cytokine production
Evidence: In a Senegalese cohort, women with Sh infection produced lower levels of TNF-α and IFN-γ following SEA stimulation of PBMCs, while they produced more TGF-β and IL-10 and had higher expression of the apoptosis marker nuclear matrix protein, compared to men with Sh infection with comparable egg burdens and schistosome circulating anodic antigen (CAA) levels [83].

Sex hormones affect immune function and lead to differences in the immune phenotype and disease response between women and men [76,77]. Furthermore, sex differences in immune response also vary over the lifespan and with reproductive status, which is particularly pronounced in women with fluctuations due to the menstrual cycle as well as during pregnancy, breastfeeding, and menopause [78–82].
351.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that female sex is associated with distinct immune responses in Sh infection. Women with Sh infection produce lower Th1 cytokines (TNF-α, IFN-γ) and higher anti-inflammatory cytokines (TGF-β, IL-10) compared to men with similar infection intensity. This sex-based difference in cytokine production could lead to an overestimation of systemic immune activation if not properly accounted for, as hormonal influences may alter cytokine profiles. The study in Senegal highlights that women with Sh infection have a different immune response pattern, which may affect the interpretation of immune data in such individuals.

351.Option: Reinfection status is most strongly associated with severe urogenital pathology and may result in misclassification of immune response data due to transient cytokine spikes after treatment
Evidence: In a Gabonese cohort, Sh reinfection was associated with lower IL-4, IL-5, and IFN-γ levels after schistosome stimulation with AWA and SEA of PBMCs compared to those who remained uninfected 2-years after PZQ treatment, suggesting that these lower cytokine levels could possibly predict risk for reinfection [75].

In an Egyptian cohort of male children and adults, higher IL-4 levels were found in individuals who became reinfected compared to those who did not. Further, IL-4 was associated with higher egg burden and remained persistently elevated even after treatment [92].

A study in Gabon showed that plasma cytokine levels varied in individuals with prior Sh infection after two years depending on if they received treatment and how many PZQ treatment courses they received [93]. Individuals who were untreated and those who received a single dose of PZQ had higher IL-5 levels and a trend towards higher IL-13 levels in response to schistosome antigen AWA compared to people without infection at the two-year follow up who had not been treated. Individuals who cleared infection after a single dose of PZQ had the highest IFN-γ, suggesting a potential protective effect of this cytokine. Moreover, repeated treatment led to high IL-5 and low IFN-γ production in response to AWA in comparison to no or single treatment, but was not protective against Sh reinfection. The authors concluded that overall cytokine response to AWA remained altered in previously persons with Sh infection despite treatment [93].
351.Rationale: The passage discusses reinfection and its association with cytokine levels, but it does not indicate that reinfection status is the most strongly associated with severe urogenital pathology. Instead, the evidence suggests that lower Th2 cytokine levels (e.g., IL-4, IL-5) and higher IFN-γ in some cases are linked to reinfection risk, but this is not directly tied to severe pathology. The studies on reinfection focus more on immune response dynamics post-treatment rather than the severity of urogenital pathology.

351.Option: Age is most strongly associated with severe urogenital pathology and may lead to underestimation of immune response magnitude due to age-related immune senescence
Evidence: Variation in cytokine responses to Sh by age.A Zimbabwean study found elevated circulating cytokines IL-2, IL-10 and IL-23 and lower IL-4, IL-13, and IL-21 in individuals with Sh infection aged 6–60 years compared to individuals without Sh infection [72]. Cytokine patterns varied with age, with increasing IL-4 and IL-10 levels, and decreasing IL-5 levels in plasma, in those with Sh infection as age increased [72]. Notably, all participants had detectable parasite-specific antibodies, particularly IgM reflecting recent schistosome exposure, but only those with Sh eggs in urine were considered individuals with Sh infection [72]. Given the suboptimal sensitivity of urine microscopy, particularly among females, some infected participants may have been misclassified as individuals without Sh infection [73]. In agreement with studies above, this cross-sectional study shows that individuals with Sh infection have mixed Th1 /Th2 systemic cytokines across the lifespan, while Th2 cytokines dominated in uninfected people who were Sh-seropositive, indicating past exposure to Sh [72].

In another Zimbabwean study, responses to antigen stimulation varied by age of the person with Sh infection. Adolescents with Sh infection aged 15–16 years showed the highest IL-4 and IL-5 responses to SWAP stimulation of whole blood, and similarly IL-5 peaked in this age group after ConA stimulation [70]. IFN-γ levels peaked at age 13–14 years in response to generic antigen stimulation, but not SWAP stimulation. IL-10 was highest in serum of children in response to SWAP but was lower in participants older than 14 years and decreased further with age. In contrast, stimulation with a ConA led to IL-10 levels that were lowest in children aged 6–12 years with an increase starting in aged 13–14 years, peak at age 15–16 years, and a decline in older participants though those over age 16 years still had higher levels than children aged 6–12 years [66].

In younger children, TNF-α was higher in serum of pre-school aged children with Sh infection, while IL-6 was only found to be higher in children aged 11–12 years in a second study of children aged 2–19 years [65]. Consistent with the Mutapi study above, Bustinduy et al also reported increased IL-10 in 13–14-year-olds with Sh infection compared to their age-matched counterparts without Sh infection [65].
351.Rationale: The passage discusses age-related variations in cytokine responses but does not establish age as the most strongly associated factor with severe urogenital pathology. While cytokine profiles change with age, the evidence does not directly link age to the severity of pathology. The Kenyan study on TNF-α and the Senegalese study on sex-based differences provide stronger associations with pathology than age-related immune senescence.

352.Option: The vaccine's limited duration of protection allows non-targeted serotypes to re-emerge and dominate after vaccine immunity wanes
Evidence: Phase III clinical trials demonstrated that the three inactivated EV-A71 vaccines showed over 90% efficacy against EV-A71-associated HFMD and nearly 100% efficacy in preventing severe manifestations of the disease
The EV-A71 vaccine was introduced in Guizhou Province in 2016, and the vaccination rate ranged from 0.96% to 9.86% in the province between 2016 and 2023
352.Rationale: The passage indicates that the EV-A71 vaccine has high efficacy and has been in use since 2016. The continued circulation of other serotypes is not attributed to waning immunity but rather to the lack of cross-protection and the ecological niche left by declining EV-A71 transmission.

352.Option: The vaccine alters the host microbiome in a way that promotes colonization and transmission of non-targeted serotypes
Evidence: The EV-A71 vaccine was introduced in Guizhou Province in 2016, and the vaccination rate ranged from 0.96% to 9.86% in the province between 2016 and 2023

The seroprevalence rates of CV-A6, CV-A10, CV-A16, and EV-A71 were relatively high among healthy individuals; over 70% of the participants had positive NtAbs against at least two serotype of these enteroviruses
352.Rationale: There is no mention in the passage about the vaccine altering the host microbiome. The continued circulation of other serotypes is more likely due to the absence of cross-protection and the ecological niche left by declining EV-A71 transmission.

352.Option: The absence of cross-protection allows non-targeted serotypes to fill the ecological niche left by declining EV-A71 transmission
Evidence: The predominant EV serotypes associated with HFMD have shifted continuously in recent years. From 2008 to 2012, EV-A71 was the dominant serotype among total HFMD cases, accounting for 55.56%, 56.98%, 40.44%, 48.65%, and 37.29%, respectively. Subsequently, other EVs became predominant from 2013 to 2018, with annual proportions of 62.85%, 42.28%, 69.19%, 51.48%, 72.08%, and 44.46%, respectively.
The implementation of the EV-A71 vaccine has resulted in a significant reduction of over 90% in EV-A71-associated HFMD cases in China since 2016

The antibodies induced by EV-A71 and CV-A16 do not provide protection for susceptible populations against infections caused by other EVs serotypes, which may exert selective pressure on infections from other EVs serotypes
352.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the EV-A71 vaccine does not provide cross-protection against other serotypes. This lack of cross-protection, combined with the significant reduction in EV-A71 cases, creates an ecological niche that other serotypes can fill, leading to their increased prevalence.

352.Option: The monovalent vaccine induces immune suppression in the host, increasing susceptibility to other enterovirus serotypes
Evidence: Phase III clinical trials demonstrated that the three inactivated EV-A71 vaccines showed over 90% efficacy against EV-A71-associated HFMD and nearly 100% efficacy in preventing severe manifestations of the disease

The seroprevalence rates of CV-A6, CV-A10, CV-A16, and EV-A71 were relatively high among healthy individuals; over 70% of the participants had positive NtAbs against at least two serotype of these enteroviruses
352.Rationale: The passage does not mention immune suppression as a result of the vaccine. The high efficacy of the vaccine and the presence of neutralizing antibodies against multiple serotypes suggest that the vaccine does not induce immune suppression.

352.Option: The high mutation rate of non-targeted serotypes enables them to evade immune responses generated by the monovalent vaccine
Evidence: The predominant EV serotypes associated with HFMD have shifted continuously in recent years. From 2008 to 2012, EV-A71 was the dominant serotype among total HFMD cases, accounting for 55.56%, 56.98%, 40.44%, 48.65%, and 37.29%, respectively. Subsequently, other EVs became predominant from 2013 to 2018, with annual proportions of 62.85%, 42.28%, 69.19%, 51.48%, 72.08%, and 44.46%, respectively.

The increasing infection rate of CV-A6 can be attributed to several factors. Firstly, the long-term circulation of EV-A71 and CV-A16 in the population may have led to natural immunity against these two serotypes. The implementation of the EV-A71 vaccine has resulted in a significant reduction of over 90% in EV-A71-associated HFMD cases in China since 2016. However, the antibodies induced by EV-A71 and CV-A16 do not provide protection for susceptible populations against infections caused by other EVs serotypes, which may exert selective pressure on infections from other EVs serotypes.
352.Rationale: While the passage mentions that non-targeted serotypes have increased in prevalence, it attributes this to the lack of cross-protection and the ecological niche left by declining EV-A71 transmission, not to the high mutation rate of non-targeted serotypes.

353.Option: The high frequency of genetic admixture across all sampled locations indicates that natural dispersal is sufficient to maintain connectivity among populations
Evidence: Results from the DeltaK analysis of Structure output indicated that there were most likely three genetic clusters (S1 Fig). That is, the DeltaK statistic was highest for k = 3 groups (DeltaK = 9.5) in comparison with all other possible numbers of groups (DeltaK range: 0.18 to 1.6 for k = 1, k = 2, and k = 4 through 10). Structure results showed a clear pattern of genetic admixture from all the sample populations, suggesting steady gene flow within this region (Fig 3).

Of 339 individual mosquitoes, 181 (53.4%) showed dominant haplotype membership associated with a single genetic group (represented in blue in Fig 3). Dominant haplotype membership (> 0.8) to the same group (depicted in blue in Fig 3) was observed among 84.4% of mosquitoes collected from Indiana/Mazan, 87.5% of mosquitos from Iquitos B, 75% of mosquitoes from Aucayo, and 64.5% of individuals from Barrio Florida. Mosquitoes collected from Tamshiaco and Nauta showed the greatest degree of ancestral admixture, with each of the three genetic groups represented approximately equally.
353.Rationale: The high frequency of genetic admixture actually supports the conclusion that human transportation networks drive connectivity, not that natural dispersal is sufficient. The admixture suggests gene flow between populations that would not be expected if natural dispersal were the primary mechanism, as natural dispersal is limited to short distances (rarely exceeding 100m). The admixture is more consistent with human-mediated transport of mosquitoes over longer distances.

353.Option: The high genetic differentiation in Barrio Florida, a town with minimal large-vehicle traffic, compared to low differentiation in Iquitos and surrounding towns with frequent boat traffic, indicates that human transportation networks are the primary driver of gene flow
Evidence: Barrio Florida populations, in contrast, were the most genetically isolated compared to other towns. Iquitos is the major regional transportation hub, with frequent visits of barges and other types of boats from surrounding areas, whereas Barrio Florida is a small town (pop: ~800), receiving most of its traffic from vehicles unlikely to be infested with Ae. aegypti (e.g., speed boats and small water taxis) [24].

Our results support the hypothesis that Ae. aegypti long-distance dispersal is driven by human activity, with particular emphasis on boat traffic. Low FST values between Iquitos and surrounding towns in addition to our Structure results (i.e, the observation that no single population was exclusive to a single site) suggest mosquito mobility between human settlements. The pattern of mosquito gene flow also mirrors human transportation patterns: FST values for a regional port (Iquitos B) were overall lower than FST values collected from the interior of Iquitos (Iquitos C and D), implying more population mixing between Iquitos port mosquito populations and surrounding towns than between Iquitos interior populations and surrounding towns.
353.Rationale: The high genetic differentiation in Barrio Florida, which has minimal large-vehicle traffic, compared to low differentiation in Iquitos and surrounding towns with frequent boat traffic, directly supports the conclusion that human transportation networks are the primary driver of gene flow. This is because the genetic isolation of Barrio Florida, which is connected by vehicles less likely to carry mosquitoes, contrasts with the genetic connectivity in Iquitos and surrounding towns, which are connected by high-traffic boat routes. This pattern aligns with the hypothesis that human transportation, particularly boat traffic, facilitates the spread of Ae. aegypti mosquitoes.

353.Option: The absence of significant genetic differentiation between Iquitos port sites and surrounding towns suggests that natural dispersal is not limited by distance in this region
Evidence: Our results support the hypothesis that Ae. aegypti long-distance dispersal is driven by human activity, with particular emphasis on boat traffic. Low FST values between Iquitos and surrounding towns in addition to our Structure results (i.e, the observation that no single population was exclusive to a single site) suggest mosquito mobility between human settlements. The pattern of mosquito gene flow also mirrors human transportation patterns: FST values for a regional port (Iquitos B) were overall lower than FST values collected from the interior of Iquitos (Iquitos C and D), implying more population mixing between Iquitos port mosquito populations and surrounding towns than between Iquitos interior populations and surrounding towns.
353.Rationale: The absence of significant genetic differentiation between Iquitos port sites and surrounding towns actually supports the conclusion that human transportation networks, not natural dispersal, are the primary driver of gene flow. Natural dispersal is limited to short distances (rarely exceeding 100m), so the lack of genetic differentiation over much larger distances (up to 500km for large barges) is more consistent with human-mediated transport. The low FST values indicate high gene flow, which is facilitated by human transportation, not natural dispersal.

354.Option: The elevated synonymous divergence in specific lectin genes is most likely due to a higher mutation rate in those genomic regions compared to the rest of the genome
Evidence: The frequency distribution of dS values for these gene pairs was plotted in R [23].

The median dS between orthologous genes of E. histolytica and E. dispar was 0.38, and 99% of dS values were below 0.73. The igl genes EHI_006980:EDI_276450 and EHI_065330:EDI_244250 and the lgl genes EHI_049690:EDI_071530 all had dS values in the top 1%, strongly supporting the hypothesis that gene conversion has occurred among these genes.
354.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the dS values in these lectin genes were in the top 1% of all pairwise comparisons, which is inconsistent with a higher mutation rate in those regions. A higher mutation rate would affect all genes in the region, not just specific genes. The passage also notes that the dS values in these genes were notably higher than for neighboring genes, which would not be expected if the entire region had a higher mutation rate.

354.Option: The elevated synonymous divergence in specific lectin genes is best explained by gene conversion between paralogous gene copies within the same family
Evidence: The pattern we observed for the igl genes EHI_006980:EDI_276450 and EHI_065330:EDI_244250 and lgl genes EHI_049690:EDI_071530, where dS was notably higher than for neighbouring genes, suggested a mechanism generating diversity other than positive selection on nonsynonymous mutations. The pattern indicated gene conversion, in which gene regions are changed to match a paralogue that may be more divergent than a gene's true orthologue, hence the elevated dS.
Phylogenetic analysis supported the hypothesis that gene conversion had made paralogous igl genes more similar than orthologous igl genes.

The frequency distribution of pairwise dS between 4531 putative orthologue pairs is plotted (grey bars). Dashed lines indicate 1st, 50th and 99th centile values. Pairwise dS values between hgl, igl and lgl positional orthologues are indicated. Both igl pairs and one lgl pair fall within the top 1% of dS values.
354.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that the elevated dS values in these lectin genes are best explained by gene conversion between paralogous gene copies. The authors explicitly state that the pattern of dS values is inconsistent with positive selection and instead supports gene conversion. The phylogenetic analysis further supports this interpretation by showing that paralogous genes cluster together rather than orthologous genes, which is a hallmark of gene conversion.

354.Option: The elevated synonymous divergence in specific lectin genes is best interpreted as evidence of horizontal gene transfer from a distantly related organism
Evidence: The genomic context of each lectin gene was viewed and synteny with E. dispar assessed to define ‘positional orthology’ (i.e. orthology defined by being in the same genomic location).

The pattern we observed for the igl genes EHI_006980:EDI_276450 and EHI_065330:EDI_244250 and lgl genes EHI_049690:EDI_071530, where dS was notably higher than for neighbouring genes, suggested a mechanism generating diversity other than positive selection on nonsynonymous mutations. The pattern indicated gene conversion, in which gene regions are changed to match a paralogue that may be more divergent than a gene's true orthologue, hence the elevated dS.
354.Rationale: The passage does not provide any evidence to support horizontal gene transfer as the explanation for the elevated dS values. In fact, the authors explicitly rule out positive selection and instead conclude that gene conversion is the most likely explanation. Horizontal gene transfer would be expected to result in genes that are more similar to genes from distantly related organisms, but the passage focuses on the relationship between paralogous genes within the same species.

354.Option: The high synonymous divergence in specific lectin genes likely results from incomplete lineage sorting, where ancestral polymorphisms have not yet fixed in the two species
Evidence: The median dS between orthologous genes of E. histolytica and E. dispar was 0.38, and 99% of dS values were below 0.73. The igl genes EHI_006980:EDI_276450 and EHI_065330:EDI_244250 and the lgl genes EHI_049690:EDI_071530 all had dS values in the top 1%, strongly supporting the hypothesis that gene conversion has occurred among these genes.

The pattern we observed for the igl genes EHI_006980:EDI_276450 and EHI_065330:EDI_244250 and lgl genes EHI_049690:EDI_071530, where dS was notably higher than for neighbouring genes, suggested a mechanism generating diversity other than positive selection on nonsynonymous mutations. The pattern indicated gene conversion, in which gene regions are changed to match a paralogue that may be more divergent than a gene's true orthologue, hence the elevated dS.
354.Rationale: The passage does not support the interpretation of incomplete lineage sorting as the cause of the elevated dS values. Incomplete lineage sorting would be expected to affect multiple genes across the genome, not just specific genes. The authors explicitly conclude that gene conversion is the most likely explanation for the elevated dS values in these specific lectin genes.

354.Option: The unusually high synonymous divergence in specific lectin genes likely reflects positive selection acting on synonymous sites to increase genetic diversity for immune evasion
Evidence: The pattern we observed for the igl genes EHI_006980:EDI_276450 and EHI_065330:EDI_244250 and lgl genes EHI_049690:EDI_071530, where dS was notably higher than for neighbouring genes, suggested a mechanism generating diversity other than positive selection on nonsynonymous mutations. The pattern indicated gene conversion, in which gene regions are changed to match a paralogue that may be more divergent than a gene's true orthologue, hence the elevated dS.

We would expect, under positive selection, that the number of nonsynonymous differences per nonsynonymous site (dN) would be high, but that dS would not differ from dS in neighbouring genes. The pattern we observed for the igl genes EHI_006980:EDI_276450 and EHI_065330:EDI_244250 and lgl genes EHI_049690:EDI_071530, where dS was notably higher than for neighbouring genes, suggested a mechanism generating diversity other than positive selection on nonsynonymous mutations.
354.Rationale: The passage explicitly rules out positive selection as the explanation for the elevated dS values. The authors note that under positive selection, dN would be high but dS would not differ from neighboring genes. Instead, they observe that dS is notably higher than for neighboring genes, which is inconsistent with positive selection and instead supports gene conversion as the mechanism.

355.Option: Pathogenic Leptospira spp. avoid detection by neutrophils through downregulation of surface lipopolysaccharide, reducing immune activation and NET formation

362.Option: Use bone marrow aspiration combined with skin biopsy to achieve the highest possible sensitivity for detecting asymptomatic carriers in a controlled clinical setting

Evidence: The combination of 2 different mucosal swab samples showed frequencies of positive results as follows: nasal and conjunctival swabs, 90% (56/62); nasal and oral swabs, 93% (26/28); oral and conjunctival swabs, 86% (24/28) (Figure 1).

Evidence: The frequencies of positive results were as follows: nasal swab, 87% (54/62); conjunctival swab, 76% (47/62); skin biopsy, 81% (50/62); bone marrow biopsy, 90% (56/62). These cPCR results were compared considering paired samples.

Evidence: The frequencies of positive results were as follows: nasal swab, 87% (54/62); conjunctival swab, 76% (47/62); skin biopsy, 81% (50/62); bone marrow biopsy, 90% (56/62). These cPCR results were compared considering paired samples.

363.Option: Use a normalized, weighted scoring system that sums category scores and divides by the maximum possible score to prevent bias from data volume or overlap

This approach directly addresses the challenge of integrating diverse and overlapping data sources by normalizing scores relative to the maximum possible, thereby preventing bias from data volume or overlap. It ensures that the final score reflects the relative strength of evidence across categories, maintaining the integrity of the consensus score.

Evidence: To derive an overall country evidence consensus score, the scores for all evidence categories were summed, and then divided by the maximum possible score and multiplied by 100.

371.Option: Use a statistical model that incorporates spatial, temporal, and meteorological covariates to estimate the counterfactual mosquito abundance in the absence of intervention
Evidence: The goals of the current work were two-fold. First, we developed a framework to model Ae. aegypti populations at a fine scale through space and time. Second, we used that framework to explore the impact of a city-wide vector control program by the Ministry of Health in Iquitos.

Our model had to account for both biotic and abiotic drivers of mosquito population dynamics and provide counter-factual estimates of what mosquito populations might have looked like had spraying not been conducted.

The final model structure is given in Fig 4. Below, the model structure is broken down in its constituent parts with each part describing the covariates used, as well as the way in which they enter the full model.

To account for this variation while avoiding enforcing any particular functional form to these relationships, flexible splines were incorporated for both abiotic spatial and abiotic temporal drivers of variation.
The remainder of this section, therefore, focuses exclusively on the negative binomial version of the models.
The over-dispersion parameter of the negative binomial base model, θ, was estimated to be 0.13.
The fitted offset in an intercept and offset-only negative binomial model was 2.03 (1.99–2.08 95% CI). This value was used in all subsequent negative binomial models.
The full model contained all spatial and meteorological covariates. The final model then added the intervention data.
The model development section describes the use of generalized additive models (GAMs) to account for spatial and temporal variation.
The model incorporated distributed lag non-linear models (DLNMs) to account for the complex relationships between meteorological covariates and mosquito abundance.
The intervention term was added to the model to estimate the effect of spraying on mosquito abundance.
The model was used to estimate the expected number of mosquitoes caught if there was no intervention.
The model was used to assess the impact of space spraying campaigns conducted by the Iquitos Ministry of Health.
The model was used to estimate the difference between the observed number of mosquitoes caught and the expected number caught if there was no intervention.
The model was used to identify site-specific drivers of mosquito abundance across different spatial and temporal scales.
371.Rationale: The passage describes a comprehensive statistical modeling approach that incorporates spatial, temporal, and meteorological covariates to estimate the counterfactual mosquito abundance in the absence of intervention. This approach is essential to distinguish intervention effects from natural fluctuations in mosquito abundance, especially when data collection is sparse and spatial heterogeneity is high. The model accounts for the complex relationships between mosquito abundance and various environmental factors, and provides counter-factual estimates of what mosquito populations might have looked like had spraying not been conducted. This approach is necessary because mosquito abundance is highly heterogeneous in space and time, and it is difficult to parse the impact of an intervention from natural variations in mosquito populations. The model uses generalized additive models (GAMs) and distributed lag non-linear models (DLNMs) to account for the complex relationships between meteorological covariates and mosquito abundance. The model also incorporates an offset to account for changes in collection methods over time. The model was used to estimate the expected number of mosquitoes caught if there was no intervention, and to assess the impact of space spraying campaigns conducted by the Iquitos Ministry of Health. The model was also used to estimate the difference between the observed number of mosquitoes caught and the expected number caught if there was no intervention. The model was used to identify site-specific drivers of mosquito abundance across different spatial and temporal scales. This approach is essential to distinguish intervention effects from natural fluctuations in mosquito abundance, especially when data collection is sparse and spatial heterogeneity is high.

372.Option: The presence and density of Aedes aegypti and Aedes albopictus populations in the region

372.Rationale: While the presence of vector mosquitoes is necessary for transmission, the question asks about the critical factor for the biological strategy's potential. The passage emphasizes Wolbachia's ability to block DENV in Ae. aegypti, not the vector density itself. The strategy's success depends more on Wolbachia's effectiveness and compatibility than on existing vector density.

372.Option: The ability of Wolbachia to suppress viral replication in laboratory cell lines and mosquito models

372.Rationale: While laboratory evidence is foundational, the critical factor for real-world application is whether Wolbachia can function effectively in wild mosquito populations. The passage notes that Wolbachia's antiviral effect is well-documented in models, but the strategy's success hinges on factors like genetic compatibility and CI-driven population replacement, not just in vitro efficacy.

372.Option: The availability of resources to conduct large-scale mosquito release programs
Evidence: Both strains produce cytoplasmic incompatibility (CI), which facilitates replacement of the wild populations through this reproductive manipulation
However, the exact mechanism which causes this antiviral effect is not fully understood
372.Rationale: Resource availability is a logistical consideration, but the passage focuses on biological mechanisms (e.g., CI, viral blocking) as the core determinants of the strategy's potential. The critical factor is whether the Wolbachia-mosquito interaction can sustainably suppress DENV, not the availability of funding or infrastructure.

372.Option: The genetic compatibility between Wolbachia strains and the host mosquito species in the region

Wolbachia is not a natural symbiont of Ae. aegypti, which is the primary vector of DENV

372.Rationale: The passage emphasizes that Wolbachia's effectiveness depends on its compatibility with the host mosquito. For example, Wolbachia's suppression of pelo is specific to female Ae. aegypti but not in D. melanogaster, highlighting the importance of genetic compatibility. CI ensures Wolbachia spreads in wild populations, but this requires the strain to be compatible with the local mosquito species. Without compatibility, the strategy cannot establish or sustain Wolbachia in the vector population, rendering it ineffective for dengue suppression.

While elevated IgG and IgE levels do indicate an immune response, the study clearly shows that these are accompanied by a general suppression of other key cytokines. The presence of specific antibodies does not negate the overall immune suppression observed in the study. The correlation with egg count actually supports the idea that immune responses are being modulated by the parasite, not that suppression is not occurring.

The study actually shows that while both sexes show suppression, the degree of suppression varies by sex, particularly with females showing more reduction in cytokines at similar infection intensities. This variation suggests that host factors (like sex) do influence the immune response, which contradicts the claim that immune suppression is independent of host factors.

Excluding co-infection with soil-transmitted helminths, individuals with C. sinensis still had significantly higher antigen-specific IgG and IgE levels, and diminished serum levels of IL-1β, IL-2, IL-4, IL-12p70, IL-17A, IFN-γ and TNF-α (Fig 7).

391.Option: Dengue transmission in Port Sudan is driven by human water storage behavior that creates persistent breeding sites, with seasonal climate shifts modulating transmission timing through changes in storage practices and vector survival

391.Option: Dengue transmission in Port Sudan is primarily driven by seasonal climate variability, with mosquito breeding occurring in response to rainfall and temperature fluctuations independent of human water storage practices
Evidence: The study shows dengue transmission in Port Sudan is autochthonous and related to storage of drinking water.

The main containers for indoor breeding of dengue vector in Port Sudan were clay pots and barrels. Owing to shortages of drinking water, the residents of Port Sudan usually preserve drinking water in these containers in close proximity to their houses.

A total of 2297 out of 4640 water containers (49.4%) were found positive for Ae. aegypti. However, over 70% of the positive containers were covered with lids, and 98% of these were located indoors. Clay-pots (in Arabic “Zeirr”) followed by plastic barrels represented the key breeding containers for pupae of Aedes mosquito, containing 63% (number of pupae=3959) and 26% (number of pupae=1651) of pupae, respectively.
The study shows dengue transmission in Port Sudan is autochthonous and related to storage of drinking water.
The disappearance of the dengue vector (Ae. aegypti) in September demands further research to define whether there is a true disappearance and if so how and from where the vector is reintroduced.
391.Rationale: The study explicitly links dengue transmission to human water storage practices, not just seasonal climate variability. The main breeding containers are described as human-made water storage vessels (clay pots and barrels) used due to water shortages, not natural water bodies. The study also notes that the vector disappears in September, suggesting that human water storage practices are a key factor in maintaining breeding sites, not just climate fluctuations.

391.Rationale: The study explicitly states that the main breeding containers are indoor, human-made water storage vessels (clay pots and barrels) used for drinking water, not outdoor natural water bodies. 391.Option: Dengue transmission in Port Sudan is driven by a single, continuous breeding cycle sustained by year-round water storage, with transmission peaks occurring in July and December due to increased human mobility and travel during holidays
Evidence: The temporal pattern of dengue in Port Sudan showed a bi-cyclic trend. Hence, the city was likely subject to two transmission peaks: the first short peak in the winter (i.e. extending for 2 months in November and December), and a second long peak in the summer (i.e. 3 months from June to August).

The two serological peaks of IgM in July and December and were preceded by the lowest and highest relative humidity during the previous months June and November (30% and 67%), respectively (Figure 7).
The highest monthly minimum temperature in July (31°C) was coincided with the peak of IgM seroprevalence (8.14%).
The study shows dengue transmission in Port Sudan is autochthonous and related to storage of drinking water.
The disappearance of the dengue vector (Ae. aegypti) in September demands further research to define whether there is a true disappearance and if so how and from where the vector is reintroduced.
391.Rationale: The study describes a bi-cyclic pattern with two distinct transmission peaks (November-December and June-August), not a single, continuous breeding cycle. The July peak is associated with high temperature (31°C) and low humidity (30%), not increased human mobility and travel during holidays. The study also notes that the vector disappears in September, suggesting that the breeding cycle is not continuous year-round.

393.Option: The negative correlation between neutrophil and platelet counts and levels of TNF-α, IL-6, IL-27, and IL-10, indicating that systemic inflammation directly causes hematological deterioration
Evidence: Hematological disorders, such as hemoglobin concentration, negatively correlated with TNF-α, IL-6, IL-27 and IL-10 (all p ≤0.0005); Platelet and eosinophil counts were negatively correlated with TNF-α, IL-6, IL-27 and IL-10 (p ≤0.0005). Neutrophil count negatively correlated with TNF-α, IL-27 and IL-6 (p ≤0.0005).
393.Rationale: While the negative correlation between hematological parameters and cytokine levels is documented, this finding alone does not establish a direct causal relationship between systemic inflammation and hematological deterioration. Correlation does not imply causation, and the passage does not provide evidence that the cytokines directly cause the hematological changes. Other factors, such as the direct effects of the parasite on bone marrow or the immune system's response to the parasite, could also contribute to these changes.

393.Option: Elevated IL-6 levels in severe VL patients, particularly those who died, and their strong correlation with clinical markers of disease severity
Evidence: We observed a strong correlation between IL-27 and IL-6 (r = 0.69), IL-10 (r = 0.85) and TNF-α (r = 0.86) (Fig 2).

Further stratification of the cytokine profiles to allow comparison between classical VL and severe VL presentations revealed that IL-6 levels were more significantly elevated in the patients with severe VL (Fig 3A; p = 0.03). Remarkably, severe VL patients that died had IL-6 serum levels of 11,443 ± 15,388 pg/ml, whereas the surviving severe VL patients had 2,848 ± 7,512 pg/ml (Fig 3B; p = 0.01). All of the patients that died had circulating IL-6 levels ≥ 200 pg/ml, while only 2 patients that survived (1 SVL, 1 classical VL) presented with IL-6 levels above this threshold.

IL-6 can directly inhibit TNF-α production [47] and also suppress Th1 responses [48] or induce Th2 responses [49]. Thus, the strong association of IL-6 with disease severity and death might be explained in two steps: firstly, through the inhibition of TNF-α in the early phase of infection, and later, by its inhibitory effect on Th1 responses [7].
393.Rationale: The evidence directly supports the hypothesis that systemic inflammation contributes to disease severity. The elevated IL-6 levels in severe VL patients, particularly those who died, and their strong correlation with clinical markers of disease severity provide a direct link between systemic inflammation and disease progression. The passage also explains the mechanisms by which IL-6 could contribute to disease severity, such as inhibiting TNF-α production and suppressing Th1 responses. This makes IL-6 a strong candidate for a direct role in the pathogenesis of severe VL.

393.Option: The strong positive correlation between IL-10 levels and both spleen/liver enlargement and elevated liver enzymes, despite IL-10 being an anti-inflammatory cytokine
Evidence: Spleen and liver enlargement, the classical manifestation of VL, both strongly and positively correlated with the circulating levels of IL-10, IL-6, IL-27 and TNF-α (all p≤0.0005). Similarly, serum levels of ALT and AST, which are also considered as markers of disease severity, positively correlated with TNF-α, IL-27 and IL-10 (p ≤0.0005).

IL-10 can protect tissues from collateral damage caused by excessive inflammation [43], but in infectious diseases, where an inflammatory response is required to prevent parasite proliferation, IL-10 may prevent an ideal response [36]. Several studies have associated the presence of IL-10 in the serum, PBMCs, spleen, liver or lymph nodes of VL patients with disease severity (reviewed in [15]) and previous studies reported an inverse correlation of IL-10 levels with parasite loads in bone marrow [44] and the blood [8].
393.Rationale: While the positive correlation between IL-10 levels and spleen/liver enlargement and elevated liver enzymes is documented, the passage suggests that IL-10 may be a response to excessive inflammation rather than a direct cause of disease severity. The anti-inflammatory nature of IL-10 suggests that it may be attempting to mitigate tissue damage rather than contributing to it. The passage also notes that IL-10 may prevent an ideal immune response, but this is more of an indirect effect rather than a direct contribution to disease severity.

Evidence: We observed strong correlations between IL-27, IL-10, IL-6 and TNF-α. 394.Rationale: The passage explicitly discusses the use of the deceleration of growth parameter (p) in modeling the epidemic curve. 395.Option: The lower culling rate in Sikka compared to Manggarai reduced the perceived risk of rabies, leading to higher vaccination uptake in Sikka
Evidence: Relatively more dog owners in Sikka vaccinated their dogs than in Manggarai, whereas in Manggarai the proportion of dog owners that had culled dogs was higher than in Sikka. This difference reflects the different approaches used by the regencies to implement control measures. The animal health authority of Sikka has focused on vaccination of dogs as the main approach to control rabies in the regency, which is in line with the national campaign. Whereas the authority in Manggarai implemented culling of roaming dogs as an additional control measure alongside the national campaign.

The culling of free roaming dogs was, however, less acceptable for the local community in comparison to the vaccination campaign [4]. This resulted in unintended negative consequences [39] in which the dog owners hid or moved their dogs to another village during the incubation phase of rabies [9,40]. In this context, the OIE and other international animal health related organizations (e.g. WHO, WSAVA, and GARC) do not recommend culling as a rabies control measure [27,41,42,43]. Culling (i.e. the killing of dogs regardless of their health status) is not effective in controlling rabies [44] and can be counterproductive [43], as previously vaccinated dogs may also be culled.
395.Rationale: The passage indicates that culling in Manggarai was an additional measure, but it was less accepted by the community and led to negative consequences like hiding dogs. The lower culling rate in Sikka does not reduce perceived risk; instead, the focus on vaccination in Sikka likely increased uptake. The statement incorrectly implies that lower culling reduces perceived risk, which is not supported by the passage.

Religious and village leaders in Sikka participated actively in encouraging dog owners to vaccinate their dogs, whereas this was not the case in Manggarai.
395.Rationale: While the study found an association between keeping female dogs for breeding and vaccination uptake, the passage does not provide evidence that this factor was more prevalent in Sikka. The disparity is more directly linked to regional strategies and community engagement, not the proportion of female dogs for breeding.

The difference in vaccination uptake between the regencies might also be due to the intensity of local community support for the control campaign [45]. The vaccination campaign in Sikka coincided with the national celebration of World Rabies Day on 8th October 2012 in Maumere. During this celebration, religious and village leaders were invited to join the event to share experiences on rabies control measures in their villages.

Uptake of the vaccination campaign was four times more likely for dog owners living in a village with good infrastructure than for those living in more remote villages with a poor road infrastructure. This is an interesting finding, as this factor has not been studied before. The accessibility of a village might be related to the transfer of knowledge from animal health authorities (especially the distribution of vaccination schedule information).

In the univariable analysis, knowledge of rabies and its control measures was significantly associated with the 2012 vaccination uptake by dog owners. However, in the multivariable analysis this association was no longer significant. This indicates that the level of rabies knowledge did not have a direct effect on the uptake of the 2012 vaccination campaign.
395.Rationale: The passage directly states that regional control strategies, including community engagement and infrastructure access, were key factors in the disparity in vaccination uptake. Despite similar knowledge levels, differences in how the campaigns were implemented (e.g., community support, accessibility) led to different outcomes. 396.Option: The spatial distribution of dengue incidence is primarily driven by human population density, with weather conditions acting as a secondary modifier of transmission risk
Evidence: The best classification criteria minimized misclassification rate and achieved high sensitivity and specificity.

The combined criteria: mean temperature >22°C for 7 or more weeks per year and minimum temperature >14°C for 33 or more weeks per year provided a classification of districts with 95% sensitivity and 95% specificity, and overall 5% misclassification rate (Fig 2).
Annual temperature range per district was a predictor of cumulative incidence magnitude.

Cumulative incidence rate was strongly associated with upper and lower limits of annual range of mean and minimum temperature across districts using linear regression, p<0.0001 in each scenario (Fig 3).
396.Rationale: The study explicitly demonstrates that weather conditions, particularly temperature and humidity, are the primary drivers of dengue transmission potential and incidence patterns. The binary classification tree results show that temperature and humidity thresholds are the most effective predictors of transmission, with high sensitivity and specificity. The study also notes that human population density and reporting rates may influence observed incidence but are not the primary drivers of the spatial patterns.
